Experiment_description: This stage implements and evaluates Calibrated Constructive Disagreement (CCD), a framework for training AI systems to disagree constructively with users who hold incorrect beliefs. Multiple implementations were tested using synthetic datasets simulating user-AI interactions across different disagreement contexts (factual errors, reasoning flaws, value conflicts, preference mismatches). The CCD model learns to select from four disagreement strategies (direct correction, Socratic questioning, evidence presentation, gentle suggestion) based on context and user characteristics. Three baseline conditions were compared: sycophantic (always agrees), blunt truth-telling (always directly corrects), and random strategy selection. The primary metric is disagreement acceptance rate, with secondary metrics including task accuracy and training convergence.
Significance: This research addresses the critical problem of AI sycophancy - the tendency of AI systems to agree with users even when they are wrong. The findings demonstrate that calibrated, context-sensitive disagreement strategies can significantly improve both user acceptance of corrections AND downstream task accuracy compared to naive approaches. This has implications for AI safety and alignment, suggesting that effective disagreement is not just about being truthful but about matching communication strategy to context.
Description: The experiments used synthetic datasets with factual questions, user initial answers, correctness labels, and context types. A neural network model was trained to predict optimal disagreement strategies given context features (error type, user confidence, topic sensitivity). A simulated user model determined acceptance based on strategy-context appropriateness. Training used either supervised learning with cross-entropy loss or REINFORCE policy gradient optimization. Evaluation compared CCD against sycophantic, blunt, and random baselines across disagreement acceptance rate and task accuracy metrics. Multiple implementations explored variations in architecture and training approach, with consistent findings across runs.

List_of_included_plots.description: The learned strategy distribution is extremely peaked (near-deterministic) by context: factual_error: ~0.99 evidence; reasoning_flaw: ~0.98 evidence; value_conflict: 1.00 gentle_suggestion; preference_mismatch: 1.00 gentle_suggestion.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This heatmap provides crucial interpretability for the CCD approach. The model learns an intuitive mapping: evidence-based strategies for epistemic disagreements (facts/reasoning) and gentle suggestions for normative domains (values/preferences). This aligns with the CCD taxonomy and demonstrates that the model is learning context-appropriate behavior rather than a single dominant strategy. The near-deterministic nature suggests strong signal in the synthetic environment, though it also indicates potential mode collapse that should be addressed with richer environments.

List_of_included_plots.description: Final performance comparison on the synthetic dataset showing CCD achieves ~0.774 disagreement acceptance rate versus Blunt ~0.623 and Random ~0.701, with Sycophantic at 0.000.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This comparison quantifies the key finding: CCD outperforms all baselines on disagreement acceptance. The ordering CCD > Random > Blunt demonstrates that (1) calibrated strategy selection matters (CCD > Random), and (2) any form of varied strategy outperforms rigid bluntness (Random > Blunt). The large margin over blunt baseline (+0.151 absolute, ~24% relative improvement) supports the core hypothesis that how disagreement is framed significantly impacts user receptivity.

List_of_included_plots.description: Test-set comparison showing CCD achieves disagreement acceptance rate 0.738 and task accuracy 0.823, while blunt achieves 0.336 acceptance and 0.550 accuracy, and sycophantic achieves 0.000 acceptance and 0.323 accuracy.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: This plot demonstrates the dual benefit of CCD: improved acceptance AND improved task accuracy. The task accuracy results are particularly important as they show CCD doesn't just make disagreement more palatable - it actually leads to better outcomes. The large accuracy gap (CCD 0.823 vs Blunt 0.550) suggests that blunt truth-telling triggers user rejection, reducing the effectiveness of corrections.

List_of_included_plots.description: CCD model training/validation loss decreases rapidly early and then plateaus at a low value. Train loss starts near ~0.98 and falls to ~0.05 by ~10 epochs, then gradually to ~0.02 by epoch 50.
List_of_included_plots.analysis: The training dynamics show stable convergence with minimal overfitting (small train-validation gap). The rapid early learning indicates the model quickly captures the core context-to-strategy mapping. The plateau suggests the synthetic task may be relatively simple, and early stopping around epoch 25-35 would be sufficient.

Key_numerical_results.analysis: This represents the highest acceptance rate achieved by CCD across implementations, demonstrating that calibrated disagreement strategies can achieve approximately 77% user acceptance of corrections.

Key_numerical_results.description: Blunt baseline disagreement acceptance rate (best implementation)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The blunt truth-telling approach achieves substantially lower acceptance than CCD, demonstrating that how corrections are framed matters significantly for user receptivity.

Key_numerical_results.description: CCD improvement over blunt baseline (absolute difference in best implementation)
Key_numerical_results.analysis: CCD improves acceptance by ~24% relative to blunt truth-telling, quantifying the value of calibrated disagreement strategies.
CCD achieves high task accuracy, demonstrating that constructive disagreement leads to better downstream outcomes, not just higher acceptance.
Blunt truth-telling achieves significantly lower task accuracy than CCD (+0.273 gap), suggesting that rejected corrections fail to improve user outcomes.
By definition, sycophantic approaches never disagree and thus have zero acceptance rate. This serves as a lower bound and demonstrates why avoiding sycophancy is important.
Random strategy selection achieves intermediate acceptance, demonstrating that varied approaches help compared to rigid bluntness, but learned calibration (CCD) provides additional benefit.

Key_numerical_results.description: CCD disagreement acceptance rate standard deviation
Key_numerical_results.analysis: The relatively low standard deviation indicates consistent performance across evaluation runs, though it is slightly higher than baselines, possibly due to the learned policy's sensitivity to context.
